evals.name: setup-overnight-copilot-loop
evals.prompt: I want to leave my Copilot coding agent running overnight on this Python project. The spec is to build out a small REST CRUD service for `todos` with sqlite — about 8 endpoints, validation, tests, and a Dockerfile. I have specs/01-todos.md ready but no scaffolding around it. Help me set up a Ralph-style loop that drives the coding agent to keep building until everything's done, but don't let it burn through my entire daily message budget.
evals.expected_output: Walks through: confirm goal/agent command/budget, run init.py with --adapter copilot or --adapter generic, move existing spec into .ralph/specs/, dry-run, then start. Explains that the Copilot adapter is a coding-agent wrapper placeholder and the operator must point command at their actual Copilot entrypoint. Calls out the LLM-call-minimizing defaults (max_iterations=20, iteration_timeout_seconds=1800), the DONE/STOP/PAUSE controls, .ralph/ being added to .gitignore on invocation, structured events.jsonl logging, and that commits/checkpoints are opt-in by default. Does NOT invoke other skills.

evals.name: switch-to-codex-cli
evals.prompt: I already have a .ralph/ workspace set up but the team wants to switch from Claude Code to OpenAI Codex CLI. What do I change?
evals.expected_output: Identifies that only .ralph/config.json's `command` field needs to change to something like ["codex", "exec", "{prompt}"], and that `stdin_from_prompt` stays false. Mentions running `--dry-run` to verify. Optionally points at references/cli-adapters.md for the exact incantation.

evals.name: diagnose-runaway-loop
evals.prompt: My ralph loop ran 18 iterations overnight on a simple bug fix and didn't write DONE. Every iteration takes 12 minutes and looks like it's mostly reading files. Help me figure out what's wrong.
evals.expected_output: Walks through the anti-patterns playbook for runaway LLM consumption: check ticket size (probably too small), check whether plan.md / gotchas.md / specs/ are present and good, check that PROMPT.md's efficiency rules survived prompt rot, inspect a recent iter-*.out to see what the agent actually did. Recommends lowering max_iterations further, batching reads, or splitting into multiple shorter loops.

evals.name: parallel-mode-for-typescript-port
evals.prompt: I have a Go service I'm porting to TypeScript, file by file, about 60 files. Could ralph loop parallelize across multiple workers so I don't have to wait for one giant loop?
evals.expected_output: Confirms this is a good fit (fan-out shape, mostly disjoint files), walks through bootstrapping two workspaces, sharing specs/ via symlink or copy, partitioning the 60 files between workers, running on separate branches with auto_commit, and merging at the end. Flags that LLM costs double — speedup is wall-clock only.

evals.name: missing-or-vague-spec
evals.prompt: I want to set up a ralph loop to 'modernize' my Express app — clean up callbacks, add types, that kind of thing. Where do I start?
evals.expected_output: Pushes back on the spec quality before doing anything else. 'Modernize' is unverifiable; the loop will drift. Walks the user through writing concrete acceptance criteria (e.g., 'every route handler uses async/await with no callback nesting'; 'tsc --strict exits 0'; 'no any types in src/'). Only then offers to scaffold. Cites SKILL.md's 'spec quality is paid up front' principle.

evals.name: spawn-supervised-subagents
evals.prompt: I have 30 independent cleanup tickets and want Ralph to spawn subagents instead of running one worker forever. What should I do?
evals.expected_output: Explains that Ralph subagents are supervised child Ralph worker loops, not host-specific chat-tool subagents. Recommends partitioning tickets/files first, then using `python /path/to/ralph-loop/scripts/ralph.py --subagents 3` or setting `subagents.count` in .ralph/config.json. Mentions per-worker workspaces under .ralph/subagents/agent-N, inherited command/timeouts, logs in events.jsonl and subagents-summary.json, and warns that conflicting edits are still the operator's responsibility.
